metadata.title: What's a parent to do? Measuring cultural logics of parenting with computational text analysis

abstract: Leading theories on parenting in the United States suggest that parenting varies widely by socioeconomic status, with middle-class parents practicing "concerted cultivation"-marked by parents' intensive efforts to foster their children's development-and working-class parents engaging in the "accomplishment of natural growth"-with children given more freedom to manage their own time. While frequently inferred that these parenting practices reflect different cultural logics of parenting, such logics are inherently hard to measure. Our paper proposes a new inductive way to study parenting logics using computational text analysis applied to a nationally representative survey where respondents provided parenting advice across three hypothetical parenting situations. Analyzing this advice using Biterm Topic Modeling we find that nearly all parenting logics reflect some form of intensive parenting, but within that are multiple nuanced versions varying across two dimensions: (1) assertive vs negotiated parenting, and (2) pedagogic vs pragmatic parenting. Using fractional multinomial logistic regression, we find little difference in how parenting logics vary by race/ethnicity, education, and income, suggesting more similarity across groups and more variability within groups than commonly understood. These findings also demonstrate how computational techniques may provide complementary tools to enrich the study of long-standing questions in social science research, at times offering an analytical naïveté that human coding cannot offer.

paragraphs: "Intensive," "helicopter," "tiger," and "free-range" are just a few of the myriad popular labels used to describe parenting approaches in the United States (US), each presented with pros and cons, as "good" or "bad" parenting, as "too much" or "too little" parental involvement. Scholars across a range of disciplines have contributed to the study of parenting approaches, outlining how they vary (or not) across social classes and racial groups, and highlighting their potential to shape children's outcomes over the life course. These findings are highly relevant given that the study of parenting lies at the core of long-standing questions in the social sciences related to changing family composition and how the institution of the family contributes to narrowing versus widening socioeconomic inequalities.

Within sociology, the dominant framework of parenting has focused on two main approaches to raising children (Lareau, 2002(Lareau, , 2011)), which vary by parents' socioeconomic status (SES). Middle-and upper-class parents engage in a process of "concerted cultivation," drawing out children's talents and skills by engaging in intensive parenting and investing extensively in material and non-material (e.g., time) resources. By contrast, working-class and lower-SES families rely on the "accomplishment of natural growth," whereby children's development unfolds more spontaneouslyas long as basic comfort, food, and shelter are providedand children are given more freedom to manage their own time and preferred activities. Subsequent studies have largely built on this foundational work, either by extending or critiquing it (e.g., Chin and Phillips, 2004;Calarco, 2014;Ishizuka, 2019).

Yet much of this research has also highlighted our limited understanding of the extent to which differences in parenting styles are driven by inequalities in resources enabling particular parenting practices, or the extent to which they reflect differences in the underlying logics that parents rely upon to guide their actions and behaviors (Weininger et al., 2015;Cheadle and Amato, 2011). 1 One recent study found that, when parents were asked to rate on a scale both a concerted cultivation and a natural growth parenting approach, parents across all SES and racial groups rated concerted cultivation higher, suggesting much more similarity in beliefs about what is good parenting vis-à-vis the actual practices enacted (Ishizuka, 2019).

Our study seeks to shed additional light on the underlying cultural logics of parenting in the US. Existing parenting research has primarily adopted three methodological approaches: small-scale ethnographic and interview studies from selected contexts and geographical areas (e.g., Hays, 1996;Lareau, 2002;Chin and Phillips, 2004), surveys with predefined measures of self-reported parenting practices based largely on the aforementioned qualitative work (e.g., Cheadle and Amato, 2011;Redford et al., 2009), and content analysis of expert parenting advice (e.g., Wrigley, 1989;Quirke, 2006). This work complements the above literature by adopting an approach that employs computational techniques to study parenting advice provided by parents. The value of this approach relies on its inductive and theory-generating nature, while drawing on large-scale nationally representative data. By abstracting from pre-existing categorizations of parenting, our method and analysis offer promising avenues for generating new insights that are unlikely to arise from research building more directly on existing popular theoretical parenting frames.

The study proceeds as follows. We first outline the contributions of our work in light of existing parenting research. We then describe how recently-developed methods from computational social science (CSS) can be leveraged to measure culture by analyzing text as data (Mohr et al., 2020;Grimmer et al., 2022;Bonikowski and Nelson, 2022;van Loon, 2022;Macanovic, 2022). We use Biterm Topic Models (Yan et al., 2013) to analyze open-ended text responses on parenting advice provided by parents in a nationally representative study of US adults (Ishizuka, 2019). Lastly, we extract relevant features that help us classify responses into theoretically meaningful categories (what we label "logics"), and we use fractional multinomial logistic regression to investigate the extent to which the identified parenting logics vary by race/ethnicity, education, and income categories. 2In so doing, our study makes three interrelated contributions to research and knowledge on families and parenting. First, by focusing on parenting advice, we capture what parents think would be good parenting. While this approach does not entirely circumvent the "resources vs culture" debateas parenting logics can themselves be shaped by one's resources and previous life experiences and economic opportunitiesparenting logics are less constrained by material resources than actual parenting actions and behaviors. While practices themselves are important for tracing the consequences of parenting, studying the underlying cultural logics of parenting is critical for understanding what leads to the adoption of specific parenting styles (i.e., their drivers). Second, our study leverages a large, nationally representative sample, yet the way we make use of these data is unconstrained by pre-existing categorizations of parenting styles typical of most closed-ended survey questions. We analyze open-ended strings of text and employ a method that inductively draws out patterns within the textual data. While we abstract from existing categorizations of parenting, we recognize that our findings are heavily influenced by how the hypothetical situations were constructed, i.e., by study design. Finally, we demonstrate how textual analysis provides a suitable approach to analyze responses from an existing survey where respondents provided parenting advice across three hypothetical parenting situations. While CSS has been only limitedly applied to the study of the family and its implications for socioeconomic inequalities (Edelmann et al., 2020), we demonstrate the great potential of computational methodologies for shedding light on long-standing social-science research questions that are hard to answer by relying on "traditional" research methods only.

sections.header_raw: Parenting styles: Approaches to raising children
sections.header_norm: parenting styles approaches to raising children
paragraphs: Parenting is an essential part of family research. How parents raise their kids is understood to be critical for the well-being of both parents and children, representing the first source of socialization for children, shaping how children learn to interact with institutions of society, and affecting their long-term mobility and life chances (Bourdieu, 1986;Lareau, 2002Lareau, , 2011;;Bodovski and Farkas, 2008;Calarco, 2014). Broadly, how parents do their parenting has been described as cultural norms (Calarco, 2014;Ishizuka, 2019;Nomaguchi and Milkie, 2020), approaches (Lareau, 2002(Lareau, , 2011)), and styles (Baumrind, 1967;Maccoby and Martin, 1983;Power, 2013;Doepke and Zilibotti, 2017). We use the label "parenting styles" here to refer to all these descriptions of parenting (Cano, 2022), which broadly encapsulate both actions and beliefs about parenting, includingbut not limited to -"parenting logics" (the focus of this paper, discussed below).

Within sociology, the most influential study of parenting was conducted in the 1990s by Lareau (2002Lareau ( , 2011)). Through a detailed ethnographic study, she found that most middle-class parents engage in a style of parenting she described as "concerted cultivation," which entails deliberately facilitating children's cognitive and social development. In contrast, she observed that working-class and poor parents were more likely to follow the "accomplishment of natural growth," a strategy emphasizing children's spontaneous (rather than guided) development. These childrearing approaches are reflected in how parents schedule their children's time outside of school and speak, teach, and academically engage with their children. A conclusion from Lareau's study, based on following both white and Black American families, was that social class is more salient than race for understanding parents' behaviors and views towards childrearing.

Other work has refined and, in some cases, challenged these findings. Overall, research has found an increasing trend towards more intensive parenting in the United States, with parents investing more heavily in their children in terms of time, money, and energy (Hays, 1996;Faircloth, 2014;Nomaguchi and Milkie, 2020). One line of this work focuses on parental investments of time spent with children, which have increased significantly in the United States. During the early 2000s, the gap between high-and low-educated parents' time investment in child care activities widened, primarily driven by the increasing absence of fathers in households with low-educated mothers (Altintas, 2016). Similar SES-based gaps in time use have been found in both summer and during the school year (Gershenson, 2013;Hastings and LaBriola, 2021). Some studies have also observed different patterns in parental investments of time by child's age. Highly educated mothers invest more time in basic care and play when youngest children are infants or toddlers than when children are older, leading to differences across education groups in basic care and play time across younger age groups (Kalil et al., 2012). Accounting for class differences in family structure, patterns of assortative mating, and specialization between partners more than doubles education-based gaps in total parental developmental childcare as compared to maternal time alone (LaBriola and Schneider, 2021). However, other recent research found that the educational disparity in developmental child care narrowed somewhat after 2003 among young children, driven largely by increases in time with children among parents with less than a college degree and among mothers not employed full time (Cha and Park, 2021;Prickett and Augustine, 2021). A related line of work focuses on financial parental investments. Over the past several decades, parental spending on items such as childcare, schooling, and extracurricular activities has increased significantly, particularly among middle and upper class families, resulting in wide class gaps as higher-SES parents seek to help their children get ahead (Amorim, 2022;Kornrich and Furstenberg, 2013;Kornrich, 2016;Schneider et al., 2018).

A third line of work has focused on parental involvement in terms of the type of activities that parents carry out with their children. Studies using the Early Childhood Longitudinal Study, Kindergarten Class of 1998-1999 observed significant differences by both social class and race (even after controlling for SES) in parental self-reports of activities with children (Cheadle, 2009;Cheadle and Amato, 2011). Chin and Phillips (2004) observed differences in how families from different ethnic and social-class backgrounds organize child care and other activities during summer vacation. In contrast to studies finding class differences in parental activities, Sui-Chu and Willms (1996) did not find that low-SES parents were less frequently involved in their children's schooling than higher-SES parents, and this finding held across a variety of measures of parental involvement. However, Calarco (2014Calarco ( , 2020) ) noted how the nature of parental involvement in school contexts and children's education varies by social class.

Parenting scholarship in developmental psychology has taken a different approach to measuring parenting styles. The foundational work is Baumrind's theory of parenting, which suggests a close relationship between the type of parenting style adopted and children's behaviors (Baumrind, 1967(Baumrind, , 1991)). Together with Maccoby and Martin (1983), this scholarship identified four parenting stylesauthoritarian, authoritative, permissive, and neglectfuleach characterized by the degree of responsiveness (the extent to which parents are warm and sensitive to their children's needs) and demandingness (the extent of control parents place on their children in an attempt to shape their behavior). In this framework, the "neglectful" parenting style is characterized by low responsiveness and low demandingness, "authoritarian" is low responsiveness and high demandingness, "permissive" is high responsiveness but low demandingness, and "authoritative" is both high responsiveness and high demandingness. This body of work generally suggests that the authoritative parenting style is the most effective and the one that is most conducive to independent, self-reliant, and socially competent kids by combining warmth, sensitivity, positive reinforcement, and limit-setting. These parenting styles have been validated across various contexts (Robinson et al., 1995) and widely applied in psychological research on children's outcomes (for a review, see Power, 2013; for an example, see Bi et al., 2018; and for a similar take from economics, see Doepke and Zilibotti, 2017). Another study on parenting styles in Australian mothers found that upper-class mothers reported more inductive reasoning and parenting "consistency" (i.e., setting clear rules and working with children to ensure they are followed), but there were no class differences in parenting warmth and anger (Cano, 2022).

A conclusion from Lareau's study, based on following both white and Black American families, was that social class is more salient than race for understanding parents' behaviors and views towards childrearing.

Other work has refined and, in some cases, challenged these findings. Overall, research has found an increasing trend towards more intensive parenting in the United States, with parents investing more heavily in their children in terms of time, money, and energy (Hays, 1996;Faircloth, 2014;Nomaguchi and Milkie, 2020). One line of this work focuses on parental investments of time spent with children, which have increased significantly in the United States. During the early 2000s, the gap between high-and low-educated parents' time investment in child care activities widened, primarily driven by the increasing absence of fathers in households with low-educated mothers (Altintas, 2016). Similar SES-based gaps in time use have been found in both summer and during the school year (Gershenson, 2013;Hastings and LaBriola, 2021). Some studies have also observed different patterns in parental investments of time by child's age. Highly educated mothers invest more time in basic care and play when youngest children are infants or toddlers than when children are older, leading to differences across education groups in basic care and play time across younger age groups (Kalil et al., 2012). Accounting for class differences in family structure, patterns of assortative mating, and specialization between partners more than doubles education-based gaps in total parental developmental childcare as compared to maternal time alone (LaBriola and Schneider, 2021). However, other recent research found that the educational disparity in developmental child care narrowed somewhat after 2003 among young children, driven largely by increases in time with children among parents with less than a college degree and among mothers not employed full time (Cha and Park, 2021;Prickett and Augustine, 2021). A related line of work focuses on financial parental investments. Over the past several decades, parental spending on items such as childcare, schooling, and extracurricular activities has increased significantly, particularly among middle and upper class families, resulting in wide class gaps as higher-SES parents seek to help their children get ahead (Amorim, 2022;Kornrich and Furstenberg, 2013;Kornrich, 2016;Schneider et al., 2018).

A third line of work has focused on parental involvement in terms of the type of activities that parents carry out with their children. Studies using the Early Childhood Longitudinal Study, Kindergarten Class of 1998-1999 observed significant differences by both social class and race (even after controlling for SES) in parental self-reports of activities with children (Cheadle, 2009;Cheadle and Amato, 2011). Chin and Phillips (2004) observed differences in how families from different ethnic and social-class backgrounds organize child care and other activities during summer vacation. In contrast to studies finding class differences in parental activities, Sui-Chu and Willms (1996) did not find that low-SES parents were less frequently involved in their children's schooling than higher-SES parents, and this finding held across a variety of measures of parental involvement. However, Calarco (2014Calarco ( , 2020) ) noted how the nature of parental involvement in school contexts and children's education varies by social class.

Parenting scholarship in developmental psychology has taken a different approach to measuring parenting styles. The foundational work is Baumrind's theory of parenting, which suggests a close relationship between the type of parenting style adopted and children's behaviors (Baumrind, 1967(Baumrind, , 1991)). Together with Maccoby and Martin (1983), this scholarship identified four parenting stylesauthoritarian, authoritative, permissive, and neglectfuleach characterized by the degree of responsiveness (the extent to which parents are warm and sensitive to their children's needs) and demandingness (the extent of control parents place on their children in an attempt to shape their behavior). In this framework, the "neglectful" parenting style is characterized by low responsiveness and low demandingness, "authoritarian" is low responsiveness and high demandingness, "permissive" is high responsiveness but low demandingness, and "authoritative" is both high responsiveness and high demandingness. This body of work generally suggests that the authoritative parenting style is the most effective and the one that is most conducive to independent, self-reliant, and socially competent kids by combining warmth, sensitivity, positive reinforcement, and limit-setting. These parenting styles have been validated across various contexts (Robinson et al., 1995) and widely applied in psychological research on children's outcomes (for a review, see Power, 2013; for an example, see Bi et al., 2018; and for a similar take from economics, see Doepke and Zilibotti, 2017). Another study on parenting styles in Australian mothers found that upper-class mothers reported more inductive reasoning and parenting "consistency" (i.e., setting clear rules and working with children to ensure they are followed), but there were no class differences in parenting warmth and anger (Cano, 2022).
sections.preview_head: Parenting is an essential part of family research. How parents raise their kids is understood to be critical for the well-being of both parents and children, representing the first source of socialization for children, shaping how children learn to interact with institutions of society, and affecting their long-term mobility and life chances (Bourdieu, 1986;Lareau, 2002Lareau, , 2011;;Bodovski and Farkas, 2008;Calarco, 2014). Broadly, how parents do their parenting has been described as cultural norms (Calarco, 2014;Ishizuka, 2019;Nomaguchi and Milkie, 2020), approaches (Lareau, 2002(Lareau, , 2011)), and styles (Baumrind, 1967;Maccoby and Martin, 1983;Power, 2013;Doepke and Zilibotti, 2017).

sections.paper_id: Hastings and Pesando - 2024 - What's a parent to do Measuring cultural logics of parenting with computational text analysis
paragraphs: Despite extensive research on parenting styles, it remains unclear the extent to which the findings documented above reflect differing cultural logics of what makes for good parenting, or if they reflect differences in the (financial and non-financial) resources required to enact the parenting styles that parents believe would be best. Most existing research on parenting has focused on parenting behaviors, and the drivers of these are either inferred by the researcher or based on self-reported explanations of behaviors given by the research subjects themselves. In discussing these tensions, researchers have referred to non-resource explanations as being tied to "cultural logics" (e.g., Lareau, 2002;Bennett et al., 2012;Aurini et al., 2020), "strategies" (e.g., Bodovski and Farkas, 2008;Cheadle and Amato, 2011), "philosophies and values" (e.g., Chin and Phillips, 2004), "cultural conceptions" (e.g., England and Srivastava, 2013), and "cultural orientations" (e.g., Weininger et al., 2015). Hereafter, we use the label "cultural logics" to refer broadly to all of these, acknowledging the possibility of further nuanced distinctions between them.

Attempts to understand parenting logics have taken a few forms. Qualitative ethnographic and interview studies have focused on the qualifications that parents themselves attribute to their own parenting styles. For example, Lareau's (2002Lareau's ( , 2011) ) seminal work found that both middle-and working-class families stated that they believed their type of parenting was preferable. However, in pushback, Chin and Phillips (2004) noted that parents expressed similar desires to help children develop or cultivate their skills and talents. They argued that different parenting styles are largely the result of parents' differential access to a wide range of resources, including money, the human capital to know how best to assess and improve children's skills, the cultural capital to know how best to cultivate children's talents, and the social capital to learn about, and gain access to, specific programs and activities. Similarly, Bennett et al. (2012) found that parents from different class backgrounds expressed largely similar desires for their children to participate in structured activities, and that the class gap in children's participation reflected the different number of community organizations organizing such activities (typically lower in low-SES neighborhoods). In the Canadian context, Aurini et al. (2020) also found that affluent parents similarly enrolled their children in after-school activities, but their stated rationale for doing so was less stratification-oriented than in the US.

Quantitative analyses of survey data have sought to address this issue by controlling for different types of resources (e.g., income, work hours, etc), and then focus on differences in parenting practices. For example, one study found that after controlling for income, wealth, work schedule, family composition, and neighborhood quality, there was a significant class gap in children's extracurricular participation by mother's education (Weininger et al., 2015). Another study found that, after controlling for income and work hours, higher-educated mothers spent more time in child care than lower-educated ones (England and Srivastava, 2013). Both studies point to cultural logics as a plausible explanation for these differences, but also acknowledge that these logics are not directly observed, and that parental education could also proxy for other resources (e.g., skills, social capital, etc) that lead to the adoption of different parenting practices.

Yet other scholars have addressed the same puzzle by examining expert advice in parenting magazines. For example, Wrigley (1989) looked at changes in attitudes toward the intellectual development of infants and young children in the US from 1900 to 1985 by hand-coding over one thousand articles from magazines drawn from the popular literature directed towards parents. She documented a broad upward shift in parental concerns towards their children's cognitive development and a general tendency for parents to place more value on formal education as a tool to promote children's life chances. A similar analysis of parenting magazines and articles in Canada explored the changing ethos of parenting in more than 500 articles, documenting a larger preoccupation about "parenting" as a deliberate undertaking (Quirke, 2006). These findings reveal a shift in emphasis from "fun" activities to an increasing focus and concern about schooling and children's cognitive development. Hays (1996) also conducted content analysis on a selection of parenting manuals which provided evidence of increasingly intensive parenting. These analyses are valuable to identify the parenting logics recommended by experts, yet they do not tell us the extent to which these are mirrored by parents' logics, nor can they provide insights on whether parenting logics differ by race/ethnicity or social class.

Lastly, in one of the most explicit attempts to separate parenting logics from parenting practices, a recent study relying on a nationally representative survey experiment found that when parents were shown hypothetical parenting scenarios, they consistently rated more intensive parenting as "better" parenting, regardless of their own race/ethnicity or social class (Ishizuka, 2019). This finding suggests that parents' attitudes and beliefs about parenting ("logics") are far more similar across social classes and racial groups than their actions or behaviors ("practices"). Nonetheless, a potential limitation of this work is that it adopts the predefined parenting-style categories of concerted cultivation and accomplishment of natural growthwhich were themselves inferred primarily from parenting behaviors. In so doing, the work finds similarity in attitudes and beliefs across groups, yet it sticks to, and adapts, the dichotomous categorization of parenting styles that is prevalent in the sociological literature on the topic.

sections.paper_id: Hastings and Pesando - 2024 - What's a parent to do Measuring cultural logics of parenting with computational text analysis
sections.header_raw: A computational approach to study parenting
sections.header_norm: a computational approach to study parenting
paragraphs: One of the most significant developments in social science research has been the growth of computational social science and, in particular, the development of computational methods to analyze large quantities of text as data (Edelmann et al., 2020;Macanovic, 2022). While analyzing text has long been important to social science, a large array of computer-assisted methods have been developed that create new and diverse avenues for research (Bonikowski and Nelson, 2022;Grimmer et al., 2022;van Loon, 2022). In particular, sociologists have noted how these methods can be especially useful when applied to measuring different cultural constructs (Mohr et al., 2020). However, family and inequality dynamics are areas of study where the presence of computational social science has been rather limited thus far (Edelmann et al., 2020). 3 With the right data, CSS methods enable an approachnot previously possibleto measure the cultural logics of parenting.

In this study we employ topic modeling, a class of CSS methods developed to uncover a set of topics whereby each document holds proportional membership in each topic. These models have been widely used in the social sciences as a way of uncovering underlying systemic patterns within texts (for an extensive review, see Boyd-Graber et al., 2017; for examples in sociology, see Fligstein et al., 2017;DiMaggio et al., 2013;Heiberger et al., 2021;Light and Odden 2017;Nelson 2021). Specifically, we use Biterm Topic Models, a topic modeling approach that is more appropriate for short texts relative to traditional topic modeling alternatives (Yan et al., 2013).

3 One notable exception is the Fragile Families Challenge, a scientific mass collaboration designed to measure and understand the predictability of life trajectories and family-related outcomes (Salganik et al., 2019), though this effort largely centered on using machine learning to improve predictive power. Another exception is a study that applies LDA methods to classify parenting actions (Rauh and Renée, 2023).

We apply these methods to a nationally representative dataset (described below) in which parents provided advice by answering open-ended questions about how other parents should act in hypothetical everyday parenting situations. These data are uniquely different from those analyzed in any parenting study described above. First, as the data record advice provided by parents, the recommended behaviorin contrast to actual behavioris not directly constrained by any type of material or immaterial resource. In other words, these data capture how parents think other parents should parent, which is what we define as the cultural logic of parenting. While material resources may shape parenting logics themselves, our focus on parents' advice to other parents minimizes the influence that material constraints may have on the adoption of specific behaviors which parents themselves may view as undesirable. This said, it is important to recognize that the way the hypothetical situations were designed may importantly affect the kind of parenting logics that emerge. This is well exemplified, for instance, in recent work showcasing important variation along race/ ethnicity in terms of how Black parents address situations where their child may face consequences of racial hierarchies such as interactions with police officers (Delale-O 'Connor et al., 2020).

Second, because this advice is provided through an open-ended questionand we analyze it as suchour analysis does not begin with predefined categories of parenting. This approach differs from some existing research on the topic, which builds on pre-existing knowledge of parenting styles, which is in turn largely influenced by parenting practices. This is an important contribution, showcasing the potentialand broader applicabilityof computational methods to move beyond popular theoretical frames on parenting.

We interpret the topics obtained from our models using both quantitative metrics typically adopted in studies of this kind and a careful reading of the strings of text pertaining to each topic. This combined approach allows us to assess whether the different topics emerging from the data appear to reflect heterogeneous cultural logics of parenting. Lastly, to explore whether differences in parenting logics vary across respondents' racial/ethnic groups, education, and income, we employ fractional multinomial logit (FML) models, a set of models where the dependent variable can belong proportionally to multiple categories with the sum of the proportions being equal to 1.
We therefore address three related research questions:

1. What can we infer about parenting logics using a computational approach to examining open-ended responses provided by parents when confronted with hypothetical parenting situations? 2. Do parenting logics align with the parenting styles discussed in the relevant literature (e.g., concerted cultivation, natural growth, authoritative, neglectful, etc.)? 3. Do parenting logics vary across socioeconomic and sociodemographic groups?

Specifically, we use Biterm Topic Models, a topic modeling approach that is more appropriate for short texts relative to traditional topic modeling alternatives (Yan et al., 2013).

3 One notable exception is the Fragile Families Challenge, a scientific mass collaboration designed to measure and understand the predictability of life trajectories and family-related outcomes (Salganik et al., 2019), though this effort largely centered on using machine learning to improve predictive power. Another exception is a study that applies LDA methods to classify parenting actions (Rauh and Renée, 2023).

We apply these methods to a nationally representative dataset (described below) in which parents provided advice by answering open-ended questions about how other parents should act in hypothetical everyday parenting situations. These data are uniquely different from those analyzed in any parenting study described above. First, as the data record advice provided by parents, the recommended behaviorin contrast to actual behavioris not directly constrained by any type of material or immaterial resource. In other words, these data capture how parents think other parents should parent, which is what we define as the cultural logic of parenting. While material resources may shape parenting logics themselves, our focus on parents' advice to other parents minimizes the influence that material constraints may have on the adoption of specific behaviors which parents themselves may view as undesirable. This said, it is important to recognize that the way the hypothetical situations were designed may importantly affect the kind of parenting logics that emerge. This is well exemplified, for instance, in recent work showcasing important variation along race/ ethnicity in terms of how Black parents address situations where their child may face consequences of racial hierarchies such as interactions with police officers (Delale-O 'Connor et al., 2020).

Second, because this advice is provided through an open-ended questionand we analyze it as suchour analysis does not begin with predefined categories of parenting. This approach differs from some existing research on the topic, which builds on pre-existing knowledge of parenting styles, which is in turn largely influenced by parenting practices. This is an important contribution, showcasing the potentialand broader applicabilityof computational methods to move beyond popular theoretical frames on parenting.

We interpret the topics obtained from our models using both quantitative metrics typically adopted in studies of this kind and a careful reading of the strings of text pertaining to each topic. This combined approach allows us to assess whether the different topics emerging from the data appear to reflect heterogeneous cultural logics of parenting. Lastly, to explore whether differences in parenting logics vary across respondents' racial/ethnic groups, education, and income, we employ fractional multinomial logit (FML) models, a set of models where the dependent variable can belong proportionally to multiple categories with the sum of the proportions being equal to 1.

We therefore address three related research questions:

1. What can we infer about parenting logics using a computational approach to examining open-ended responses provided by parents when confronted with hypothetical parenting situations? 2. Do parenting logics align with the parenting styles discussed in the relevant literature (e.g., concerted cultivation, natural growth, authoritative, neglectful, etc.)? 3. Do parenting logics vary across socioeconomic and sociodemographic groups?
sections.preview_head: One of the most significant developments in social science research has been the growth of computational social science and, in particular, the development of computational methods to analyze large quantities of text as data (Edelmann et al., 2020;Macanovic, 2022). While analyzing text has long been important to social science, a large array of computer-assisted methods have been developed that create new and diverse avenues for research (Bonikowski and Nelson, 2022;Grimmer et al., 2022;van Loon, 2022). In particular, sociologists have noted how these methods can be especially useful when applied to measuring different cultural constructs (Mohr et al., 2020).

sections.paper_id: Hastings and Pesando - 2024 - What's a parent to do Measuring cultural logics of parenting with computational text analysis
Hastings and Pesando - 2024 - What's a parent to do Measuring cultural logics of parenting with computational text analysis
paragraphs: This project uses publicly-available data from a survey conducted with a nationally representative sample of parents in the US. Details about this study are described in Ishizuka (2019). The data were collected as part of a survey experiment supported by the Time-sharing Experiments for the Social Sciences (TESS). The survey was online, with the probability-based sample drawn from an online panel. Respondentswho were all parents themselveswere given different vignettes and asked how parents should respond to their children in specific situations of daily life involving a parent with a child between the ages of 8 and 10. Each respondent received only one vignette, and each vignette received about 600 responses. 4 The question that forms the object of this study elicited an open-ended response which had a mean length of 20 words and a median length of 16 words. Additional details about the survey, including the exact wording of the question and differences by respondent education, race/ethnicity, and gender, are provided in the supplemental appendix. We focus on three parenting situations where the advice provided varied substantially, potentially capturing heterogeneity in parenting logics. These three vignettes well align with the three domains suggested by Lareau (2002) as places where parenting logics and styles might vary: (i) the organization of leisure ("Bored After School"), (ii) language use ("Bedtime Rules"), and (iii) interaction with institutions ("Take Your Medicine"). The organization of children's leisure refers to children's participation in formal and informal activities, and parents' involvement in those activities. Language use relates to the nature of parents' verbal interactions with children. Finally, interaction with institutions refers to how families relate to professionals in institutions such as schools or medical establishments. 5 The vignettes randomly manipulated the gender of the parent (based on the parent's name) and the child. Table 1 shows the vignettes for each situation, further described below, and the number of respondents who received that vignette, with sample parent names and child gender manipulations. Responses are presented below in our results.

To explore heterogeneity across socioeconomic and sociodemographic groups, we focus on three key variables collected in the survey: race/ethnicity, educational attainment, and income. Race/ethnicity includes five categories, namely "White non-Hispanic," "Black non-Hispanic," "other non-Hispanic," "2+ races non-Hispanic," and "Hispanic." Due to low shares of individuals in the categories "other non-Hispanic" and "2+ races non-Hispanic," we collapsed these two categories into one, thus analyzing four categories. For educational attainment, the survey provides four categories: "less than high school," "high school," "some college," and "bachelor's and above." Again, due to low shares in the first category, we collapsed the former two categories into "high school or less," thus analyzing three categories overall. Income is provided as a 19-category variable (median income about $60,000/yr) from which we created four income quartiles. The survey also includes respondents' age and gender, which we include as controls in our models.

sections.paper_id: Hastings and Pesando - 2024 - What's a parent to do Measuring cultural logics of parenting with computational text analysis
paragraphs: We start our analysis by cleaning and preprocessing responses and analyzing them with topic models. Having the potential to help researchers uncover specific cultural elements (Bail, 2014), topic modeling has been extremely useful for examining open-ended responses (Roberts et al., 2014;Pietsch and Lessmann, 2018;Miner et al., 2023). We employ Biterm Topic Models, an approach more appropriate for analyzing shorter texts relative to alternative topic models (Yan et al., 2013). We draw upon several metrics to determine the preferred number of topics, ultimately settling on four topics (Mimno et al., 2011;Roberts et al., 2014). We interpret the topics using quantitative measures alongside a careful reading and interpretation of the text itself (Sievert and Shirley, 2014;Grimmer et al., 2022). Finally, we employ FML regression to model how race/ethnicity, education, and income are associated with the observed parenting logics. Additional details on each step are provided below.

sections.paper_id: Hastings and Pesando - 2024 - What's a parent to do Measuring cultural logics of parenting with computational text analysis
paragraphs: Preprocessing: We follow standard practices by replacing all capital letters with lowercase, removing punctuation and stop words (common English connectors such as "and, the", and "that"), and stemming the words (discarding the end of words so as to remove suffices and combine words with a common root). 6 We also removed words that appeared in the description of the situation itself or in a large portion of the responses (e.g., we removed "bore,""child,""kid,""school,""daughter," and "son" from responses to the Bored After School situation) and created a document-term matrix in which each row represents a document (i.e., one of the responses for a situation) and each column represents a word (cell ij = 1 if word j appears in document i or 0 otherwise). 7 Biterm Topic Models: The most popular topic model approach is Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) (Blei et al., 2003). Broadly, this method uses the co-occurrence patterns of words within documents to generate via Bayesian hierachical models a set of topics where each document is a mixture of topics. Through this iterative process, LDA identifies the words that are most frequently associated with each of the topics specified and produces a set of topic weights assigned to each document, which represent the extent to which the document belongs to a particular topic (Wallach et al., 2009;Grimmer et al., 2022).

For short documentssuch as what we have herethe document-term matrix is quite sparse, and LDA (as well as popular variations, such as structural topic modeling (STM)) does not perform well (note that we did estimate topic models with both LDA and STM and found conceptually similar results). An extension of the conventional topic model is the Biterm Topic Model (BTM), which we employ in this paper (Yan et al., 2013). BTM uses word co-occurrence patterns ("biterms") within the entire set of documents (in our case, all responses to one situation) to then model the distribution of topics. As such, it is preferable for modeling short texts, and has been applied to analyzing open-ended survey responses (Pietsch and Lessmann, 2018). 8 We implement BTM using the "BTM" package in R (Wijffels, 2021). 9 In the analysis presented here, we analyzed each of the responses from each situation separately. We also explored all three responses together. However, given that the inductive process is based on word co-occurrences, we found in this alternative approach that the topics overwhelmingly reflected which situation a response came from.

sections.paper_id: Hastings and Pesando - 2024 - What's a parent to do Measuring cultural logics of parenting with computational text analysis
paragraphs: "When Kevin's daughter is diagnosed with asthma, the doctor says the medication might help. Kevin's daughter says: 'I don't want to take medicine'. How would you advise Kevin to respond in this situation?" 535 6 Similar results were found by lemmatizing the words instead of stemming. 7 The topics we found without removing the words that appeared in the situation description were similar across situations. However, across all the topics the most prevalent words that respondents used were the contents of the situation themselves, which was not helpful for interpreting the topics. We also examined results when dropping documents with less than three words (less than 5% of documents) and found overall similar results. 8 We also tested a newer alternative method to BTM for short texts, stLDA-C ("single topic Latent Dirichlet Allocation with clustering") that has recently been developed by Tierney et al. (2021). Code and details are available at https://github.com/g-tierney/stLDA-C_public. We found similar results for the topics. stLDA-C conceptualizes each document as being restricted to belong to a single topic and, as indicated by the method's name, the method can also cluster respondents. However, in our data we only have one document (i.e., one piece of advice) per respondent, so we cannot utilize the respondent-clustering feature here. 9 The BTM package is available at https://github.com/bnosac/BTM.

Selecting the number of topics (k): An important decision in topic modeling is selecting the number of topics. Broadly, the goal is to have a sufficient number of topics to capture variation across documents, while also avoiding creating an overly complex model with too many topics to be useful and, foremost, interpretable (Grimmer et al., 2022). If parenting styles reflect parenting logics, then the dominant theoretical framework would suggest two topics (k = 2) that may align with the concerted cultivation and accomplishment of natural growth parenting styles. Drawing on this theoretical background, we examined a two-topic solution and explored the corresponding results. Upon examining key words and carefully reading responses within each topic, we did not find that results fit well with the dichotomous framework.

Topic modeling also offers quantitative metrics to provide guidance on the preferred value(s) of k. We follow the direction of Roberts et al. (2014) of seeking to maximize both exclusivity and semantic coherence. Further details on these metrics, as well as challenges in selecting k, are provided in the Appendix. Scholars caution that these metrics do not necessarily provide a recommendation as to the single best model. Still, we found we needed four topics to approach the "coherence-exclusivity frontier," and we found the 4-topic model, presented here, to be readily interpretable and to outperform both a 2-topic and a 3-topic solution (see Appendix). It is also important to note that there may not even be a "best" k for any particular situation, nor is it necessarily the case that each scenario should have the same number of topics. Models with a few topics might better capture generalized parenting logics, while models with many topics may capture specific nuances within logics.

Interpreting the topics: We relied on several tools to interpret the topics. For each topic, we examined which words have high relevance (similar to FREX scores, which stands for both frequent and exclusive to a topicsee Sievert and Shirley (2014)) and high prevalence. 10 Then, for every topic we read all documents that had a probability of belonging to that topic of at least 0.9 (in general 10-20 documents), as well as another 10-20 randomly selected documents with a probability of at least 0.5 of belonging to that topic. Based on these results, we developed summary labels and interpreted heterogeneity across topics for each parenting situation.

We considered how topics might align (or not) with existing labels of parenting styles, including concerted cultivation and natural growth (Lareau, 2002(Lareau, , 2011)), and authoritative, authoritarian, permissive, and neglectful (Baumrind, 1967;Maccoby and Martin, 1983). We found that our topics could best be described as varying across two newly-defined dimensions or axes of analyses: (1) assertive vs negotiated parenting and (2) pedagogic vs pragmatic parenting. We describe these in our results and elaborate on how they relate to the existing literature on parenting.

Scholars caution that these metrics do not necessarily provide a recommendation as to the single best model. Still, we found we needed four topics to approach the "coherence-exclusivity frontier," and we found the 4-topic model, presented here, to be readily interpretable and to outperform both a 2-topic and a 3-topic solution (see Appendix). It is also important to note that there may not even be a "best" k for any particular situation, nor is it necessarily the case that each scenario should have the same number of topics. Models with a few topics might better capture generalized parenting logics, while models with many topics may capture specific nuances within logics.

Interpreting the topics: We relied on several tools to interpret the topics. For each topic, we examined which words have high relevance (similar to FREX scores, which stands for both frequent and exclusive to a topicsee Sievert and Shirley (2014)) and high prevalence. 10 Then, for every topic we read all documents that had a probability of belonging to that topic of at least 0.9 (in general 10-20 documents), as well as another 10-20 randomly selected documents with a probability of at least 0.5 of belonging to that topic. Based on these results, we developed summary labels and interpreted heterogeneity across topics for each parenting situation.

We considered how topics might align (or not) with existing labels of parenting styles, including concerted cultivation and natural growth (Lareau, 2002(Lareau, , 2011)), and authoritative, authoritarian, permissive, and neglectful (Baumrind, 1967;Maccoby and Martin, 1983). We found that our topics could best be described as varying across two newly-defined dimensions or axes of analyses: (1) assertive vs negotiated parenting and (2) pedagogic vs pragmatic parenting. We describe these in our results and elaborate on how they relate to the existing literature on parenting.

paragraphs: We then examined whether parenting logicsas inferred from our topic modelsvary across race/ethnicity, education, and income groups. As BTM provides a multi-membership model, each document is assigned proportionally to all topics. We model this using fractional multinomial logit models (FML, Buis, 2010). FML models a set of dependent variables where each must range between 0 and 1 and must always, for each observation, add up to 1 -most commonly proportions. 11 This is a type of fractional response model, a class of models that is related to binary response models, but instead of estimating the probability of being in one bin of a dichotomous variable, it models variables that take on all possible values in the unit interval. Further details on these models and their use in social science research are provided in the Appendix.

Our key independent variables are race/ethnicity, education, and income, as defined and categorized above. We considered models with variables together, as well as models that excluded key predictors one at a time. In all of our regression analyses we also control for the age and gender of the respondents, as well as the gender of the parent and child in the specific parenting situation. All models include survey weights and are estimated with robust standard errors. Predicted probabilities are shown in the main text, while full FML estimates, including analyses that exclude one key predictor at a time, are provided in the Appendix. 12

paragraphs: We first provide results from the Biterm Topic Models. For each of the three parenting situations, we present a table that outlines 10 The prevalence words in each topic are frequently common across documents, and thus not especially informative for interpretation. In contrast, Lift is the ratio of a term's probability within a topic to its marginal probability across all the documents. The highest-lift words can be rare words that only appear in a certain topic. Relevance is weighted average of the logarithms of a term's prevalence and its lift, and is similar to the FREX score. We utilize the LDAvis to calculate lift, by setting the weighting term (lamba) to 0.5. This paper's replication code allows one to examine relevance at other lamba values. For additional details on the methods, see Sievert and Shirley (2014). 11 For instance, in our setting a document may have a probability of 0.6 of belonging to topic 1, 0.2 of belonging to topic 2, 0.15 to topic 3, and 0.05 to topic 4. 12 Recent work by Egami et al. (2022) shows that there is a risk of insufficiently conservative standard errors when a same text data is used both for estimating topics and then making inferences using those same topics. One solution is to split the data into two parts, and use one part (the training data) to build the topic models, use this model to classify the topics in the other part (the test data), and then do the regression with only the test data. Our data includes approximately 600 documents per vignette that are rougly 20 words each, so when we randomly split the data into training and test groups as suggested, this presented several problems: (1) There is no systematic way to readily determine if the new topics based on the training data were the "same" or "similar" to the topics from our main analysis. (2) Most critically, predicting the topics in the test data requires using the word co-occurrence patterns from the training data, and since there are not a large number of words to begin with, we found that the model based on the training data was not very effective at predicting the topics in the test data. Upon inspecting the responses in the test data after they had been assigned topics, we felt the model was not able to effectively predict the topics that responses should belong to. (3) Running models on the test data only further increases the standard errors due to the smaller sample size. each topic, how prevalent it is (defined as the % of documents where the topic had the largest probability of belonging to), the most relevant words, 13 the most prevalent words (if not already among the relevant ones), and four exemplary quotes. Each quote presented had at least a 0.9 probability of belonging to the topic it represents.

In light of the heterogeneity that emerges, we classify topics along two newly-developed axes of analyses, namely whether a specific advice or recommendation is super-imposed or jointly negotiated/discussed with the childwhat we label assertive versus negotiated parentingand whether there is a pedagogic aspect embedded in parents' recommendations which involves a reflection or teaching moment shared with the child versus whether the advice is aimed at simply reaching a specific outcome irrespective of processwhat we label pedagogic versus pragmatic parenting. Our framework is summarized in the diagram provided in Fig. 1. By intersecting these two axes of analysis, we identify a broad range of parenting logics, alongside some of their features, which extends beyond the dichotomous categorization of parenting styles dominating the literature, suggesting that there is likely more heterogeneity when it comes to studying parents' cultural beliefs and attitudes regarding good parenting.

sections.paper_id: Hastings and Pesando - 2024 - What's a parent to do Measuring cultural logics of parenting with computational text analysis
paragraphs: The 'Bored After School" (BS) parenting situation captures parents' advice when a child reports being bored after school, and a summary of the findings are reported in Table 2. The topic order is arbitrary, hence topics are presented in each table from most to least frequent (also shown visually in the Appendix).

The first topic (BS-1) focuses on parents finding things to do for their children. Some of the most relevant words include "sport," "club," and "involve" (as in, "find something for [your child] to get involved in …"). While some bits of advice do suggest asking the child what they are interested in, the focus is on parents, who need to find an activityfor the most part, and something organized and extracurricularfor their children to participate in. We describe this as a pragmatic-assertive parenting logic, as the advice holds a pragmatic element (i.e., children need to be entertained by getting into a specific activity to occupy their time), top-down, and largely involves the organized, scheduled activities associated with intensive parenting.

The second most prevalent topic (BS-2) reflects a slightly different parenting logic. The topic retains the pragmatic element, as it is focused on getting children to go outside and play with other children. Yet the advice is at times assertive and at other times negotiated, with a strong focus on children using creativity and imagination. This is clear, for instance, from the following quote: "Vanessa should sit with her daughter and create a list of things that she can do after school." We thus simply label this topic as pragmatic, and across all three parenting situations this topic is the one that gets closest to Lareau's description of the parenting style of the accomplishment of natural growth. Parents' advice is for children not to waste their time (hence the pragmatism), and to fill their time by leveraging their creativity and imagination.

The third topic (BS-3) shares many similarities with the first, except that much of the advice incorporates a constructive parentchild exchange related to which type of activity may best suit the child's talent or which activity could be most constructive for the child's future, thus including a process of negotiation with the child. For example, some of the advice includes: "Find out what the child's interests are, and encourage, or provide access to, the participation in said interests." We label this pragmatic-negotiated parenting logicfinding an activity for the child remains important, but with an element of negotiation between parents and children.

The final, least prevalent topic (BS-4) also captures a parenting style that prioritizes discussion and exchange with the child in a way that is reciprocal and aimed at identifying a solution by going over a range of possibilities and examples that are not super-imposed in an assertive manner but jointly negotiated. However, in many cases the advice is not focused on finding something to do, but to help children understand why they feel as they do ("… open up a conversation with her son to ask why he feels that way.") and/or how this can be a learning moment ("Tell her to start cleaning the house. After a while of cleaning, she will start learning how to self entertain and relying on others to entertain her. This will teach them a valuable lesson for the future."). The teaching aspect is also apparent from the high-relevance word "wrong." Given the heavier focus of the advice on teaching and/or guiding the child, we describe this as a pedagogic-negotiated parenting logic. Overall, while we observe significant variation along the assertive-negotiated axis, the parenting logics emerging from this first parenting situation primarily retain a pragmatic element (over 90% of the sample).

paragraphs: The "Bedtime Rules" (BR) parenting situation captures parents' advice when a child shouts at them following the reminder that it's time to go to bed, and the results are summarized in Table 3.

The most prevalent topic (BR-1) reflects an assertive logic, as the tone is assertive and there is little parent/child negotiation, yet the approach is not unidirectional and parents place a heavy focus on the benefits and consequences that a good night sleep can bring, as evidenced by the high-relevance words "tomorrow" and "reason." Keywords and quotes are mixed when it comes to the pedagogic aspect of the advice. While some advice is thoroughly discussed with the child and outlines the benefits of specific behaviors, some other is more imposed to reach a specific goal. Two quotes illustrate the simultaneously pedagogic and pragmatic approaches embedded in this topic, respectively: (i) "I would tell the son that when he is grown he can stay up as late as he wants, but right now he has to go to bed at bedtime. If he doesn't, he'll be grumpy when he wakes up." and (ii) "Go to bed so you can go to school and once you 13 Relevance balances prevalence (common words) with lift (words that are unique to each topic). are grown you can make your own rules!" We thus simply label this topic assertive.

The second most prevalent topic (BR-2) is similar to the first, yet less pragmatism emerges from the advice, hence we label this a pedagogic-assertive parenting logic. The tone is still assertiveyet less than aboveand the discussion is highly rule-based, but there is more willingness on the part of parents to highlight how discipline early in life may serve children well in their future, thus incorporating more of a teaching aspect in their advice. This is exemplified in the advice: "Tell her son, my rules are for your protection and safety. You need your rest so you can function properly."

The third topic (BR-3) resembles BS-4, as a pedagogic aspect is combined with a non-assertive and reciprocal approach, hence pedagogic-negotiated parenting logic. Parents stress the importance of rules, alongside the importance of getting good sleep, yet adopting a warmer and more empathic approach which also exhibits a natural willingness to engage with children and understand some of the reasons why they don't want to abide by the rules. This topic summarizes the idea of coming to an agreement together, as exemplified, for instancel, by the response: "I am sorry that you dislike my rules, and when you are an adult you get to make your own rules. Why don't you want to go to bed? Is something bothering you and you want to talk about it?" Lastly, the least prevalent topic (BR-4) closely resembles BS-1, and we also label this topic pragmatic-assertive. The assertive nature is quite apparent from the high-relevance words, among which we find "establish" and "spank." The bottom line emerging from this topic is the following: rules are rules and children have to follow them. The tone is assertive and, at times, dismissive; the approach is top-down, and there is very little room for exchange and discussion with the child. One respondent suggested: "Ignore what your child says. It's typical behavior. Stick to your guns and your child will realize you make the rules and she must listen." Overall, while we observe significant variation along the pedagogic-pragmatic axis, the parenting logics emerging from this second parenting situation primarily retain an assertive element (about 80% of the sample).

sections.paper_id: Hastings and Pesando - 2024 - What's a parent to do Measuring cultural logics of parenting with computational text analysis
paragraphs: The "Take Your Medicine" (TM) parenting situation captures parents' advice when a child diagnosed with asthma refuses to take a specific medication recommended by the doctor. Results are provided in Table 4.

The most prevalent topic (TM-1) shares commonalities with BR-2, and we also label it pedagogic-assertive. Little pragmatism emerges from the advice, yet the tone is assertive and firm. The discussion is relatively rule-based, but there is willingness on the part of parents to highlight how discipline early in life may serve children well in their future, thus incorporating a teaching aspect in their advice (as also demonstrated by the high-relevance word "explain"). This is best exemplified by the following piece of advice: "Tell the son the benefits of taking that medication. Make sure he knows the medicine would not harm him."

The second topic (TM-2) exhibits a very pragmatic aspect of parental advice which distinguishes it from the first one, yet it also highlights an assertive and authoritarian dimension, hence the label pragmatic-assertive. The advice is direct and blunt, with little exchange with children. Suggestions are very much directed towards highlighting the general benefits of the medication for getting back in good health as swiftly as possible. Some elements of coercion emerge from this topicas well as some high-relevance words such as "frighten" and "struggle" -which make this parenting approach far from the negotiated parenting logics. For example: "Work long and hard to convince or coerce her to take it on her own." Differently from the above two, the third (TM-3) and fourth (TM-4) topics both reflect a negotiated parenting logic, as parents envision engaging in conversations with their children and providing their children agency to reflect upon their own decisions (e.g., the relevant words "reason," "patient," and "teach"). This parenting logic shows reciprocal exchange and discussion, alongside advice that is geared towards providing pedagogical insights that will be useful for children in their future lives. The two topics, though, 1. Schematic diagram of emerging parenting logics. exhibit marginal differences as the former blends, at times, a pedagogic approach with very pragmatic suggestions, hence the simple label negotiated. The following two quotes exemplify this mix: (i) "I would advise her that, although medications can have side effects, […] she should definitely try the medication for his well being, as asthma can be deadly and he should not be allowed to suffer unnecessarily; " (ii) "OK, there's a number of things we can do. Medicine is one of those. We can try some other things if you are willing to try first. But if you want to maintain an active lifestyle, you might have to consider it." Conversely, TM-4 most closely matches our definition of a pedagogic-negotiated parenting logic, highlighting reciprocal exchange and discussions about the benefits of medicine not just for playing but also for children's lives and wellbeing. The following quote well exemplifies it: "Talk with daughter about the importance and benefit of taking medicine and also discuss potential adverse reactions she may have if she leaves asthma untreated." Overall, TM-1 is by far the most prevalent topic, with close to 60% of respondents belonging to it, while only 6% of respondents belong to TM-4.

We can now return to our schematic representation outlined in Fig. 1 to examine the placement of each topic within the diagram. In Fig. 2 we roughly locate each identified topic in a quadrant of the plane. Note this is a stylized representation, based on our subjective interpretation of the features of each topic described above. 14 Our findings demonstrate the existence of a continuum of parenting logics, suggesting broader heterogeneity in parental attitudes and beliefs relative to actual parenting practices.

sections.paper_id: Hastings and Pesando - 2024 - What's a parent to do Measuring cultural logics of parenting with computational text analysis
sections.header_raw: Variation in parenting logics across groups
sections.header_norm: variation in parenting logics across groups
paragraphs: We conclude by using FML models to examine if the parenting logics inferred from the BTM vary by respondents' race/ethnicity, education, and income. Expectations based on previous literature focused more broadly on parenting styles are mixed. Lareau's (2002Lareau's ( , 2011) ) work suggests mostly differences by education and income, but Cheadle and Amato (2011) also document differences by race. Conversely, Ishizuka's (2019) recent work suggests that while actual parenting behaviors may or may not vary across these dimensions, what parents believe about good parenting varies little.

Given the interpretive complexity of the FML models, we present predicted probabilities from the full models in Fig. 3 coefficient estimates are reported in the Appendix. Each panel of the figure provides results from one parenting situation. The predicted probability of each line shows how the probability of the advice being in that topic varies across each characteristic, when all other variables in the model are set to their mean values. Each column shows results across situations for each characteristic (race/ethnicity, left; education, middle; and income, right). This analysis is focused on differences across characteristics, thus straight horizontal lines reflect no heterogeneity, while lines' deviations from horizontal patterns reflect differences.

Beginning with "Bored After School," we observe that White respondents were the least likely to reflect a pragmatic-assertive 14 As such, the location of each point should be considered approximate (we return to this in the discussion.).

parenting logic (although the difference is only statistically significant between non-Hispanic Whites and Hispanics), and that Whites were most likely to reflect a pragmatic logic. We observe almost no variation by education, while we find that the pragmatic parenting logic was the least popular among respondents in the bottom income quartile. Moving to the "Bedtime Rules" situation, we find that Black respondents were more likely to reflect an assertive parenting logic, although this difference is not statistically significant. By education, those with a high-school degree or less were least likely to recommend a pragmatic-assertive logic and most likely to support a pedagogic-assertive onea difference that is statistically significant. For income, those in the top income quartile were most likely to support an assertive logic, although none of the income differences are statistically significant. Finally, looking at the "Take Your medicine" situation, we find that Black respondents were more likely to reflect a pedagogic-assertive parenting logic, rather in line with the previous panel. Again, there are no significant differences by education, while those in the lowest income quartile were more likely than those in other income groups to support a pedagogic-assertive parenting logic. Taken together, these results show minimal variation by education level, while there is some variation by race and ethnicity between White and Black respondents, alongside some differences between the bottom income quartile relative to the other income groups. Interestingly, the one topic that most closely maps onto a natural growth parenting style (BS-2) is the logic that is least reflected among low-income respondents. If anything, our findings reveal higher support of pragmatic parenting logics among higher-SES individuals and higher support of pedagogic parenting logics among lower-SES individuals, suggesting once again that parenting logics may not map well onto parenting styles. For robustness purposes, we also looked at models with race/ethnicity, income, and education excluded one at a time. These results, included in the Appendix, corroborate our findings.

In supplemental analyses, we examined models where we pooled the topics across vignettes by creating variables for "any pedagogic", "any pragmatic", "any negotiated," and "any assertive" based on our topic labels. We then ran logistic regression models predicting the probability of belonging to each of these categories. These results are shown in the Appendix. Despite the increased sample size in each model (N = 1664), none of the coefficients for race/ethnicity, education, or income were significant at the p < .05 level. This corroborates our main findings. Note, however, that this approach depends on the accuracy of our labels, in order to determine which topics to pool together. In contrast, the FML models estimate the probability of being associated with each particular topic found through our topic modeling approach. While we use the labels for interpretation, the "wrong" descriptive label for a topic does not undermine the analysis of variation in parenting logics across groups.

Finally, looking at the "Take Your medicine" situation, we find that Black respondents were more likely to reflect a pedagogic-assertive parenting logic, rather in line with the previous panel. Again, there are no significant differences by education, while those in the lowest income quartile were more likely than those in other income groups to support a pedagogic-assertive parenting logic. Taken together, these results show minimal variation by education level, while there is some variation by race and ethnicity between White and Black respondents, alongside some differences between the bottom income quartile relative to the other income groups. Interestingly, the one topic that most closely maps onto a natural growth parenting style (BS-2) is the logic that is least reflected among low-income respondents. If anything, our findings reveal higher support of pragmatic parenting logics among higher-SES individuals and higher support of pedagogic parenting logics among lower-SES individuals, suggesting once again that parenting logics may not map well onto parenting styles. For robustness purposes, we also looked at models with race/ethnicity, income, and education excluded one at a time. These results, included in the Appendix, corroborate our findings.

In supplemental analyses, we examined models where we pooled the topics across vignettes by creating variables for "any pedagogic", "any pragmatic", "any negotiated," and "any assertive" based on our topic labels. This corroborates our main findings. Note, however, that this approach depends on the accuracy of our labels, in order to determine which topics to pool together. In contrast, the FML models estimate the probability of being associated with each particular topic found through our topic modeling approach. While we use the labels for interpretation, the "wrong" descriptive label for a topic does not undermine the analysis of variation in parenting logics across groups.

Expectations based on previous literature focused more broadly on parenting styles are mixed. Lareau's (2002Lareau's ( , 2011) ) work suggests mostly differences by education and income, but Cheadle and Amato (2011) also document differences by race.
