sections.paper_id: Hastings and Pesando - 2024 - What's a parent to do Measuring cultural logics of parenting with computational text analysis
paragraphs: This paper has contributed to advancing family research by measuring the cultural logics of parenting using a novel approach that leverages large-scale inductive CSS methods to analyze open-ended advice provided by a nationally representative sample of US parents. The approach is advantageous in that it does not impose any a priori parenting item or category and can flexibly and inductively accommodate any number of heterogeneous parenting logics, thus pushing theory towards a broader and unconstrained understanding of parenting. Across all situations analyzed, parents provided evidence of intensive parenting, advising that good parenting means being involved in children's lives and actively engaging in solving the issues that children face in their daily routines. Nonetheless, some parenting advice promotes more of a top-down approach (assertive), while other suggests that parenting decisions are jointly negotiated with children (negotiated). Similarly, some parenting advice is aimed at "getting to the point" (pragmatic), while other is more geared towards reflection, reasoning, and teaching valuable life skills (pedagogic).

When assessed in relation to existing types of parenting styles, the vast majority of emerging topics include elements that reflect features of the concerted cultivation approach (Lareau, 2002(Lareau, , 2011) ) (i.e., different expressions of intensive parenting). In a similar spirit, our assertive parenting logic shares commonalities with the "authoritarian" parenting style discussed in developmental psychology, in that high demandingness is combined with low responsiveness and low degrees of warmth (Baumrind, 1967;Maccoby and Martin, 1983). Not least, our negotiated parenting logic gets close to the idea of "authoritative" parenting in that high demandingness is combined with high responsiveness, setting of clear standards, high expectations, and reciprocal exchange. Conversely, none of the emerging logics reflect a "neglectful" parenting style. Despite there being a few elements of coercion emerging in rare instances, no parents' advice in our data aligns with the idea of neglecta finding which may also be shaped by the type of hypothetical parenting situations included in the study design, which do not fully capture complexity, variability, and heterogeneity in parenting. No research that we are aware of has explicitly drawn out or formalized differences between pragmatic and pedagogic parenting, nor intersected this axis of analysis with other dimensions. As such, our findings enrich the picture by highlighting two different axes of analysis which create a continuum of parenting logics not previously identified in the literature. One avenue for future research could be to develop quantitative measures of these parenting logics, so that the type of stylized representation in Fig. 2 could be replaced with more precise "locational" measures of the extent that parents hold different parenting logics.

On a broader level, our study reveals that while the dichotomous categorization may well capture heterogeneity in parenting styles, there is likely more heterogeneity when it comes to studying parents' cultural beliefs and attitudes regarding good parenting. Relatedly, while some parenting logics map well onto existing features of parenting styles (such as intensive parenting), the overall mapping is not one-to-one, corroborating the idea that parenting logics capture something "different" that does not conform well to binary categorizations.

Finally, we examined whether parenting logics inferred from our topic models varied significantly across race/ethnicity, education, and income groups. Although we found a range of heterogeneous parenting logics, we documented little differences in how these correlated with race/ethnicity, education, and income, highlighting much more similarity in parental logics across socioeconomic and sociodemographic groups relative to what would be expected based on existing research on parenting practices. As such, our findings are consistent with Ishizuka's (2019) conclusions that race and socioeconomic status are not predictive of different beliefs about what is considered "good" or "bad" parenting. 15 On top of this, our inductive approach has the potential to highlight variation in parenting logics that exists even within sociodemographic groups.

As the first of its kind, this study presents some limitations, many of which can be addressed in future parenting research. First, the nationally representative survey we are working with only provides one response per person for each specific parenting situation, thus preventing us from observing the "durability" of parenting logics across different scenarios. Collecting advice from the same respondent on multiple situations would allow us to test for the durability of parenting styles across different contextsan important element that would provide additional robustness to our results. From a methodological standpoint, data of this kind would allow us to adopt other variants of topic modeling (such as single topic Latent Dirichlet Allocation with clustering, stLDA-C) that provide an opportunity to cluster not only responses, but also individuals according to their parenting logics (Tierney et al., 2021). Relatedly, as stressed by Small and Calarco (2022), we acknowledge that uncovering meaning and motivation from respondents' answers typically requires "follow-up" or efforts to gather additional data to clarify or contextualize what initial data collection efforts reveal. As such, relying on one single answer may provide too limited a picture. However, recent research comparing topic modeling to qualtiative coding of the same texts suggests that both methods can pick up many of the same broad themes (Miner et al., 2023).

Second, computational methods for text analysis, including topic modeling and BTM, can be quite sensitive to specific decisions researchers make in the preprocessing and analysis strategies. For example, the decision to remove words that appear in the vignettes themselves is an important one. Likewise, the decision to use four topics to analyze each situation naturally results in different takeaways than would a different numberfor example, more topics would likely result in finding more dimensions of variation, which might capture more nuances in parenting logics. As such, it is essential that the technical steps that one follows to clean and analyze the data are informed and guided by solid theoretical and sociologically relevant thinking. While we have sought to do so here, we recognize other reasonable decisions may yield different results, and, to this effect, our code for preprocessing and analyzing the data is available for other researchers to use. Related to this point, there are other methods for text analysis beyond topic modeling (e. g., Kozlowski et al., 2019;Taylor and Stoltz, 2020), some of which include more deductive approaches that may be more useful for leveraging existing theories on parenting styles (e.g., van Loon, 2022;Macanovic, 2022).

Third, while our finding pertaining to similarity in parental logics across groups echoes Ishizuka (2019), we acknowledge that SES differences may remain for three reasons. First, results may be constrained by the study design, i.e., by how the different hypothetical situations were created. In other words, it may be the case that some situations presented may not fully capture the "proper" heterogeneity and complexity in parenting that parents usually face, and that, in turn, they may disproportionately capture scenarios that are more common among specific sociodemographic groups. It is likely that some situations may have been devised with little consideration of the racial/ethnic sociodemographic fabric of the sample population and that, as such, additional scenarios including topics/themes that are of particular relevance for Black or Hispanic parents may exhibit more variation in logics had they been posed to participants (Delale-O 'Connor et al., 2020). Second, our method may not be the most suitable for identifying differences. One reason behind such a possibility has to do with the nature of topic models, which are designed to identify what is common across an entire corpus of texts. 16 Alternative methods such as scoring words, clustering, and principal component analysis may be better suited to identify distinctions within a corpus. To our knowledge, none of these approaches have been used to study parenting either. Third, sample sizes are small and not even across sociodemographic characteristics (i.e., respondents are more likely to be White non-Hispanic with relatively high education), making it hard to assess whether lack of differences is "real" vs driven by low statistical power. As such, we think these analyses highlight the need for further research on the topic. Fourth, we examine heterogeneity in parenting styles using three markers of socioeconomic and sociodemographic status, namely race/ethnicity, education, and income, i. e., the ones included in the dataset. In line with existing research, we acknowledge that other important dimensions such as occupation and family structure/composition may constitute important sources of variation in parenting styles (Chan and Koo, 2011). Finally, future research could attempt to gather appropriate data for measuring both parenting logicsas we have hereand actual parenting practices (as measured in many other studies). The pervasive ideal of intensive parenting emerging from this study conflicts with the widespread SES differences that are still observed in terms of actual parenting behavior, such as differences in time investments and economic resources. As such, an important future direction will entail understanding further how and why cultural logics of parenting areor are notreflected within actual behavior. Ideally, studies measuring parenting logics, practices, and resources (economic, social, and cultural) could shed additional light on the drivers of parenting styles.

To conclude, this work has enriched our understanding of parenting in the US society by examining the cultural logics of parenting and highlighting a broad range of intensive parenting ideals, an effort that has important implications for understanding parental investments in children and, ultimately, children's school performance, later-life academic outcomes, well-being, and patterns of social mobility and inequality. Moreover, the study demonstrates how computational methods can be used to complement and extend our understanding of the functioning of the family obtained through both existing qualitative and quantitative research methods. In a period of rising inequality and increasing time and financial pressures faced by families, we see interdisciplinary and methodologically varied research as one promising pathway to move such understanding forward.
